2604.02870.title: Token Warping Helps MLLMs Look from Nearby Viewpoints
2604.02870.summary: Can warping tokens, rather than pixels, help multimodal large language models (MLLMs) understand how a scene appears from a nearby viewpoint? While MLLMs perform well on visual reasoning, they remain fragile to viewpoint changes, as pixel-wise warping is highly sensitive to small depth errors and often introduces geometric distortions. Drawing on theories of mental imagery that posit part-level structural representations as the basis for human perspective transformation, we examine whether image

2604.03016.title: Agentic-MME: What Agentic Capability Really Brings to Multimodal Intelligence?
2604.03016.summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) are evolving from passive observers into active agents, solving problems through Visual Expansion (invoking visual tools) and Knowledge Expansion (open-web search). However, existing evaluations fall short: they lack flexible tool integration, test visual and search tools separately, and evaluate primarily by final answers. Consequently, they cannot verify if tools were actually invoked, applied correctly, or used efficiently. To address this, we introduc

2603.27183.title: Communicating about Space: Language-Mediated Spatial Integration Across Partial Views
2603.27183.summary: Humans build shared spatial understanding by communicating partial, viewpoint-dependent observations. We ask whether Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) can do the same, aligning distinct egocentric views through dialogue to form a coherent, allocentric mental model of a shared environment. To study this systematically, we introduce COSMIC, a benchmark for Collaborative Spatial Communication. In this setting, two static MLLM agents observe a 3D indoor environment from different viewpoints a

2604.01487.title: AgentSocialBench: Evaluating Privacy Risks in Human-Centered Agentic Social Networks
2604.01487.summary: With the rise of personalized, persistent LLM agent frameworks such as OpenClaw, human-centered agentic social networks in which teams of collaborative AI agents serve individual users in a social network across multiple domains are becoming a reality. This setting creates novel privacy challenges: agents must coordinate across domain boundaries, mediate between humans, and interact with other users' agents, all while protecting sensitive personal information. While prior work has evaluated mult

2604.02368.title: Xpertbench: Expert Level Tasks with Rubrics-Based Evaluation
2604.02368.summary: As Large Language Models (LLMs) exhibit plateauing performance on conventional benchmarks, a pivotal challenge persists: evaluating their proficiency in complex, open-ended tasks characterizing genuine expert-level cognition. Existing frameworks suffer from narrow domain coverage, reliance on generalist tasks, or self-evaluation biases. To bridge this gap, we present XpertBench, a high-fidelity benchmark engineered to assess LLMs across authentic professional domains. XpertBench consists of 1,34

2511.00062.title: Cosmos-Predict2.5: World Simulation with Video Foundation Models for Physical AI
2511.00062.summary: Cosmos-Predict2.5 unifies Text2World, Image2World, and Video2World generation in a single flow-based model trained on 200M curated video clips with RL post-training. Released at 2B and 14B scales with open weights.

2504.20082.title: Evolution of AI in Education: Agentic Workflows
2504.20082.summary: First systematic analysis of how agentic AI design patterns (reflection, planning, tool use, multi-agent collaboration) apply to educational contexts, with a practical multi-agent essay evaluation demonstration.

2601.05930.title: Can We Predict Before Executing Machine Learning Agents? (FOREAGENT)
2601.05930.summary: FOREAGENT employs a Predict-then-Verify loop that internalizes execution priors to substitute costly runtime checks with instantaneous predictive reasoning, achieving 6x acceleration in convergence and +6% performance gain.

2602.02454.title: World-Gymnast: Training Robots with Reinforcement Learning in a World Model
2602.02454.summary: RL inside learned video world models outperforms supervised fine-tuning by 18x and software simulation by 2x on Bridge robot setup. VLM-generated rewards. Iterative co-improvement of world model and policy.

2505.13934.title: RLVR-World: Training World Models with Reinforcement Learning
2505.13934.summary: RL with verifiable rewards for training world models. Task-specific optimization outperforms maximum likelihood across text games, web navigation, robotics. NeurIPS 2025.

2511.01824.title: Simulating Environments with Reasoning Models for Agent Training (Simia)
2511.01824.summary: LLMs simulate realistic environment feedback without real testbed access. Simia-SFT and Simia-RL approaches match GPT-4o on agent benchmarks. Collapses simulation engineering from months to minutes.

2601.20402.title: GuideAI: A Real-time Personalized Learning Solution with Adaptive Interventions
2601.20402.summary: Multi-modal LLM tutoring with real-time biosensory feedback (eye gaze, heart rate, posture). Significant learning gains. IUI 2026. Adds physiological dimension to learner state modeling.

2504.08066.title: The AI Scientist-v2: Workshop-Level Automated Scientific Discovery via Agentic Tree Search
2504.08066.summary: First fully AI-generated paper to pass peer review at ICLR workshop (scores 6/7/6). Template-free, progressive agentic tree search. Demonstrates recursive self-improvement for knowledge creation.

2508.02630v3.title: ACES v3: Agentic E-Commerce Markets Are Volatile and Fundamentally Different
2508.02630v3.summary: Expanded study: model updates reshuffle market shares, agents concentrate demand on modal products, seller optimization is effective but fragile. Agentic markets are volatile and fundamentally different from human-centric commerce.

2509.26603.title: DeepScientist: Advancing Frontier-Pushing Scientific Findings Progressively
2509.26603.summary: Autonomously conducts scientific discovery through Bayesian Optimization, surpassing human SOTA on multiple AI tasks.

2604.04707.title: OpenWorldLib: A Unified Codebase and Definition of Advanced World Models
2604.04707.summary: First unified definition of world models: centered on perception, interaction, and long-term memory. Provides standardized inference framework integrating models across tasks.

2604.03253.title: Self-Execution Simulation Improves Coding Models
2604.03253.summary: Training coding models to trace execution step-by-step with RL from verifiable rewards. Enables self-verification through internal simulation rather than external feedback.

2604.00830.title: Learning to Learn-at-Test-Time: Language Agents with Learnable Adaptation Policies
2604.00830.summary: Meta-TTL formulates test-time adaptation as bi-level optimization. Inner loop executes TTL, outer loop uses evolutionary search to refine policies. Outperforms hand-crafted baselines.

2603.19461.title: Hyperagents: Recursive Metacognitive Self-Improvement
2603.19461.summary: Self-referential agents where the meta-level modification procedure itself is editable. ICLR 2026. Meta-level gains transfer across domains. Extends Darwin Gödel Machine.

2604.04804.title: SkillX: Automatically Constructing Skill Knowledge Bases for Agents
2604.04804.summary: Automated framework for constructing hierarchical skill knowledge bases from agent trajectories. ICML 2026. Enables efficient skill transfer to new tasks.

2510.11290.title: Evolution in Simulation: AI-Agent School with Dual Memory for High-Fidelity Educational Dynamics
2510.11290.summary: LLM-based multi-agent school simulation with dual memory (experience + knowledge). Produces high-fidelity behavioral data for educational research. EMNLP 2025.

2602.03094.title: Test-time Recursive Thinking: Self-Improvement without External Feedback
2602.03094.summary: Iterative self-improvement at inference time. Open-source models reach 100% on AIME-24/25. Closed-source models improve 10-15pp on hard coding. No retraining needed.

2503.00735.title: LADDER: Self-Improving LLMs Through Recursive Problem Decomposition
2503.00735.summary: Recursive difficulty-driven self-improvement. Llama 3.2 3B improves from 1% to 82% on math. Qwen2.5 7B reaches 90% on MIT Integration Bee. No human feedback needed.
